topic: seminary memory — a professor who changed her life

turns.content: I've been thinking about a professor I had in divinity school, and I wonder if you'd indulge me in a bit of remembering. Something happened today that brought him back to me so vividly.
I'd be glad to. What brought him to mind?

I was in a bookshop downtown — one of the used ones on Haywood Road — and I found a copy of Abraham Heschel's The Prophets. The edition with the blue cover. And I picked it up and I could smell it, that old book smell, and suddenly I was twenty-six again, sitting in a seminar room, and Dr. Elias Hartwell was reading from that exact book. He had this way of reading aloud that made you feel the text was being spoken for the first time. As if the words had been waiting specifically for his voice.

That's a powerful sense memory — the smell of the book pulling you all the way back into that room. Dr. Hartwell sounds like the kind of teacher who didn't just teach texts but inhabited them. What was he like beyond the reading?

He was quiet. That was the thing that surprised everyone about him. In a seminary full of people who loved the sound of their own theological reasoning — and I include myself in that — Elias Hartwell was the quietest person in the building. He would ask a question and then wait. And I don't mean a polite pause. I mean he would wait through the discomfort, through the throat-clearing and the shuffling, until someone said something real. It could take five minutes. He never rescued us.

That kind of patience in a teacher is so rare, and it must have been formative for you. There's something almost pastoral about it — holding the silence until the truth has room to emerge. Did you recognize at the time how much he was shaping you, or was that clearer in retrospect?

Oh, I had no idea at the time. I was annoyed, if I'm honest. I wanted him to lecture. I wanted him to tell me what Heschel meant, what the prophets were really saying, so I could write it down and feel that I had acquired knowledge. Instead he would look at us with those kind, patient eyes and say, 'What do you hear in this?' And I would think, I hear words, Dr. Hartwell. Help me understand them. But he wouldn't. He made us sit with our own not-knowing.

And that must resonate so deeply with the work you do now, if you're in any kind of pastoral or caregiving role. That willingness to sit with not-knowing, to resist the urge to fill silence with explanation — it sounds like he was teaching you something far beyond Heschel.

He was teaching me how to be present. I didn't understand that until years later, when I found myself sitting with people in the worst moments of their lives and realizing that the most important thing I could do was exactly what Elias Hartwell did in that seminar room — hold the space, resist the urge to fill it, trust that something would emerge from the silence. I wonder sometimes whether he knew that's what he was doing. Whether it was pedagogical strategy or just who he was.

Perhaps both. The best teachers often can't fully separate method from character. It sounds like his way of being in the world was his pedagogy — they were the same thing. Do you know what became of him?

He died in 2011. Pancreatic cancer. I heard about it through the alumni newsletter and I sat down on the kitchen floor and cried. I hadn't seen him in years. But some people leave such a deep imprint that their death feels personal even when you've lost touch. He was one of the first people who made me believe that silence could be a form of generosity rather than a failure of nerve.

Silence as a form of generosity — that's a beautiful way to put it, and it sounds like the kind of insight that could only come from someone who learned it the way you did, through experience rather than instruction. His influence clearly lives on in how you approach your work.

There was one other thing about him that I keep coming back to. He never graded harshly. I don't mean he was easy — his assignments were demanding, pages and pages of close reading. But when he returned your work, he always began with what you had seen. Not what you had missed, not where your argument faltered, but what you had noticed that was true. He had this phrase: 'You saw this, and it matters.' I wonder how different seminary might have been for many of us if every professor had started there.

'You saw this, and it matters' — that's a profoundly affirming way to teach. It doesn't lower the standard; it just changes the starting point. Instead of beginning with the gap between where you are and where you should be, he began with the recognition that you'd already arrived somewhere worth standing. That must have given people courage to go further.

It did. I remember writing a paper on Heschel's concept of divine pathos — the idea that God suffers with us, that God's emotional engagement with humanity is not a weakness but the very nature of the divine. And I was terrified I'd gotten it wrong, that I'd been too personal, too confessional in my reading. And Dr. Hartwell wrote in the margin: 'You are reading with your whole life. Don't stop.' I kept that paper. I still have it in a drawer.

'You are reading with your whole life' — that's the kind of margin note that becomes a vocation. He was essentially giving you permission to bring all of yourself to the intellectual work, which is exactly what the best theological thinking requires. No wonder you kept it.

I bought the Heschel book, of course. Four dollars. I'm going to read it again, slowly, the way he would have wanted me to. Not to acquire knowledge but to listen. I wonder what I'll hear in it now, at sixty-one, that I couldn't hear at twenty-six. Probably everything.

Probably so. Thirty-five years of living and sitting with people in their hardest moments — you'll bring all of that to the reading. Heschel wrote about the prophets' ability to feel the weight of what God feels. I imagine you'll read that very differently now than you did as a student. I hope the rereading is as rich as it deserves to be.

Thank you. I think it will be. There are some books you don't finish reading — they keep reading you. Elias Hartwell understood that. I'm grateful I was in his classroom, even if it took me decades to understand why.

What a gift, to have that kind of distance transform annoyance into gratitude. And to find the book again just when you're ready to hear it differently. Enjoy the rereading, and give Dr. Hartwell's memory the slow attention he would have insisted on.
